titleStmt.title: Postcard from George D. Roche to Nancy McCarthy, 24 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: George D. Roche

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
In this postcard to Nancy McCarthy, her uncle George thanks her for her letter. He adds that Father Bleasdale is in hospital with blood poisoning caused by some tinned food and “we feel quite lonely. No one to find fault with!”. He also passes on an address for Mdlle Raymonde Caron she requested.
Nancy McCarthy was a well-practiced letter-writer, and her collection of correspondence spans eighty years. This postcard is one of seven included in the Letters of 1916 collection written to Nancy by her uncle, George D. Roche, on active service with the 7th London regiment during World War I.

msIdentifier.repository: UCC Library Special Collections and Archives

correspAction.placeName: The Convent, Mountrath, Queen's County, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
529. La Grande Guerre 1914-15-16 — Les ruines de SOUCHEZ (P.-de-C) Au fond la cote 119
Aspect of Souchez - In gthe main the cote 119 A.R.
BL/PP/NMC/607 Miss Nancy McCarthy
The Convent
Mountrath
Ireland Easter Monday 1916 My Dear Nancy Thanks very much for your nice long &
interesting letter. I wish I cd
reply in the same way but no time for letters
just now this will have to do. You will be
concerned to learn that we have strafed
Father Bleasdale. He is in hospital near
the sea coast suffering from blood poisoning.
Something in the way of tinned food upset
him and we feel quite lonely. No one to
find fault with! Glad to see by your
sketches that you are such a hard knut
at study and that your coiffure is
adjusted accordingly. The address you
want is Malle. Raymonde Caron
Essars, pres Bethune
Pas de Calais, France Best love & good wishes from Uncle George
